channel.title: Notes on Alberto Ruiz Biestro
channel.description: Recent content in Notes on Alberto Ruiz Biestro

item.title: Topology and Wannier Functions
item.description:
It is often stated in paper abstracts that “One cannot build [[Wannier Functions]] for [[Topological Insulators]]”, and when one reads the introduction, the statement changes to “One cannot build exponentially localized [[Wannier Functions]] for [[Topological Insulators]]”. Where does this distinction come from, and is it relevant for building WFs in practice?

The insulator picture

In a paper by Prodan1 he builds on top of Kohn’s idea of [[Nearsightedness]]

SSH model

References

1.
Prodan, E. Nearsightedness of electronic matter in one dimension. Physical Review B 73, 085108 (2006).

item.title: Wannier Functions
item.description:
Definition

Wannier functions (WFs) are a dual representation of Bloch wavefunctions from the periodic potential problem. They are defined as Fourier transform’s of Bloch wavefunctions (i.e. a linear combination with plane-wave weights)

\[ {w_{n\mathbf{R}_j}}=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{\mathbf{k}}e^{-i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{R}_j}{\psi_{n\mathbf{k}}}, \]

where \(\mathbf{R}_j\) is the lattice vector, \(\mathbf{k}\) the crystal momentum, and \(n\) the band index; we assume a discrete \(\mathbf{k}\)-point mesh, and thus \(N\) represents the number of mesh points in the Brillouin Zone.
